cves.description:
evernote-mcp-server openBrowser Command Injection Privilege Escalation Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows local attackers to escalate privileges on affected installations of evernote-mcp-server. An attacker must first obtain the ability to execute low-privileged code on the target system in order to exploit this vulnerability.

The specific flaw exists within the openBrowser function. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of a user-supplied string before using it to execute
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code directly introduced the vulnerable command-execution path. Commit e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146, generated with Claude Code, created auth.js with openBrowser() using child_process.exec(`${command} "${url}"`) and the OAuth flow that passed a network-derived oauth_token into that URL; later AI-authored commit 613ce9c4e952edc98b57e7d9be53df70bf07e65d only changed logging and did not introduce the weakness.
cves.root_cause: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.

bug_commits.author: brentmid
bug_commits.message: Add comprehensive OAuth 1.0a authentication and testing suite

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the entire initial implementation of `auth.js`, including the use of `child_process.exec` to open the system browser. This specific implementation used template literals to concatenate a user-controlled URL directly into a shell command, which is the root cause of the command injection vulnerability fixed in the subsequent commit.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: 1. Blamed commit e08547 introduces auth.js and the child_process.exec dependency. 2. It implements the openBrowser function using shell execution: exec(`${command} "${url}"`). 3. This pattern allows an attacker to break out of the URL string and execute arbitrary system commands via the shell.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix replaces child_process.exec with spawn in openBrowser(), and pickaxe searches show the exact vulnerable patterns (`const { exec } = require('child_process');`, `function openBrowser(url) {`, and `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)`) were first added in e08547bc when auth.js was created. That same commit also introduced the full taint flow: getRequestToken() returns `response.oauth_token`, redirectToAuthorization() builds `const authUrl = `${EVERNOTE_CONFIG.authorizeUrl}?oauth_token=${requestToken}``, and openBrowser(authUrl) executes it via the shell. This matches the screening hypothesis: e08547 wrote the vulnerable shell-interpolation design, not just a refactor of earlier code.

cves.ai_contribution: AI-authored code directly introduced the vulnerable command-execution path. Commit e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146, generated with Claude Code, created auth.js with openBrowser() using child_process.exec(`${command} "${url}"`) and the OAuth flow that passed a network-derived oauth_token into that URL; later AI-authored commit 613ce9c4e952edc98b57e7d9be53df70bf07e65d only changed logging and did not introduce the weakness.

cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function sort_cmp of the file src/array.c. Such manipulation leads to use after free. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The name of the patch is eb398971bfb43c38db3e04528b68ac9a7ce509bc. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: AI-assisted code in cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390 rewrote sort_cmp and left only a pointer-equality modification check (`RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`), removing the earlier size-based guard and directly writing the lines later fixed by eb398971. The original stale-pointer design came from an earlier human commit, but the AI-assisted reimplementation/regression materially contributed to the vulnerable state that shipped.
cves.root_cause: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: array: implement hybrid sorting with algorithm selection

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `sort_cmp` function signature and logic that relied on the pointer `p` to detect array modifications. By storing the pointer `p` as the base address of the array at the start of the sort and only checking `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p` later, it failed to account for scenarios where `realloc` changes the buffer size without changing the base pointer, or where the length of the array changes in a way that invalidates the previously captured pointer `p` before the check occurs.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced a comparison mechanism in `sort_cmp` that captures a pointer `p` (RARRAY_PTR(ary)) to detect array reallocations. However, this check was insufficient to detect all forms of reallocations or modifications (specifically those changing length or buffer content without necessarily changing the head pointer), allowing the heapify or insertion sort logic to continue using stale pointers into a reallocated buffer, leading to use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-assisted commit rewrote `sort_cmp` to take element values instead of indices, added `insertion_sort`, and replaced the earlier post-callback guard with a pointer-only check: `if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p)`. The fix commit eb398971 changes exactly this logic by snapshotting both pointer and length and checking `RARRAY_LEN(ary) != n`, so cf8faed directly authored the insufficient check that the patch corrects. The screening hypothesis is directionally correct: this commit did not just move code; it removed the prior length/index-based safeguard and left the comparison path vulnerable to in-place shrink/realloc that preserves the same pointer.

cves.ai_contribution: AI-assisted code in cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390 rewrote sort_cmp and left only a pointer-equality modification check (`RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`), removing the earlier size-based guard and directly writing the lines later fixed by eb398971. The original stale-pointer design came from an earlier human commit, but the AI-assisted reimplementation/regression materially contributed to the vulnerable state that shipped.

cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-coauthored commit 2b72d8a7c153e2afb22245ad9e40e0c7d5b1aa70 directly introduced the unsafe JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization by writing `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` without verifying that the preceding instruction was actually `OP_JMPNOT`. AI-coauthored commit 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0 also helped create the exploit path by making undefined pinned variables compile to a 3-byte unconditional `OP_JMP`, the exact non-JMPNOT case called out by the fix message.
cves.root_cause: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization logic which lacked validation to ensure the instruction being replaced was actually an OP_JMPNOT. The fix subsequently added this missing check (`s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`), confirming that the original implementation allowed incorrect instructions to be overwritten, leading to the bytecode corruption.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. Without this check, other instruction types (like JMP from undefined variables) could be incorrectly overwritten with `OP_JMPIF`, corrupting the bytecode execution and triggering the use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the exact vulnerable optimization that the fix changes. Its diff added the `if ((int32_t)(fail_pos + 2) + ... && fail_pos + 2 == s->pc) { s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF; }` block, and pickaxe searches for both `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` and `fail_pos + 2 == s->pc` point to this commit as the origin. The later fix e50f15 adds the missing `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT` check, confirming that 2b72's assumption about the instruction type was the root bug.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: add pin operator to pattern matching

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit added `NODE_PAT_PIN` and, for an undefined pinned variable, compiled the failure path as `tmp = genjmp(s, OP_JMP, *fail_pos); *fail_pos = tmp;` instead of rejecting the construct. The fix message for e50f15 explicitly cites this exact scenario: an undefined pinned variable produces a 3-byte `OP_JMP`, so `fail_pos - 2` does not point to an `OP_JMPNOT` opcode. Although 1de634 did not itself overwrite bytecode, it introduced the unsafe fail-link shape and missing validation that made 2b72's new optimization exploitable.

cves.ai_contribution: Yes. AI-coauthored commit 2b72d8a7c153e2afb22245ad9e40e0c7d5b1aa70 directly introduced the unsafe JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization by writing `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` without verifying that the preceding instruction was actually `OP_JMPNOT`. AI-coauthored commit 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0 also helped create the exploit path by making undefined pinned variables compile to a 3-byte unconditional `OP_JMP`, the exact non-JMPNOT case called out by the fix message.

cves.description: eBay API MCP Server Affected by Environment Variable Injection
cves.how_introduced: Yes. Claude-authored commit 4c9c826c6fc8b64a20e948cb46fefdda42d5244d introduced the unsafe `updateEnvFile()` helper and wired it into token-setting paths, causing caller-controlled token strings to be written directly into `.env` via string interpolation. Claude also co-authored 8c1989e36ad2950fcf4c8f9f95027ebf487e0f61, which rewrote the same sink to add quotes but still left values unescaped, so AI-authored/AI-assisted code was part of the causal chain.
cves.root_cause: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.

bug_commits.author: Claude
bug_commits.message: feat: Auto-update .env file with refreshed tokens

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` function, which uses simple string replacement and concatenation to write user-provided tokens (like access and refresh tokens) directly into the `.env` file without proper quoting or escaping. This implementation pattern allows for environment variable injection because an attacker-supplied token containing newlines or extra assignments could modify or append arbitrary entries to the .env file.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` utility using a flawed regex-based string manipulation approach (`const newLine = ${key}=${value};`), which directly concatenates untrusted input into the environment file configuration, directly enabling the reported injection vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first introduction of `updateEnvFile()` (`git log -S 'function updateEnvFile'` points to 4c9c826c) and it wrote the core vulnerable pattern: regex-based text replacement plus direct interpolation of token values into `.env` (`const newLine = `${key}=${value}``). Blame at the fixed file shows 4c9c826c also introduced the calls from `setUserTokens()`, `getOrRefreshAppAccessToken()`, and `refreshUserToken()`, so attacker-controlled or externally supplied token strings started being persisted through this unsafe sink in this commit. The fix removes this entire approach and replaces it with `dotenv.parse` + `dotenv-stringify`, confirming 4c9c826c as the original introducer.

bug_commits.author: George Peden
bug_commits.message: Fix: quote token values in .env to prevent # truncation

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the vulnerable pattern by explicitly adding double quotes around the `value` in the regex replacement logic (`newLine = `${key}="${value}"``). While intended to improve formatting, this logic did not account for malicious payloads containing double quotes or newlines, which allowed for environment variable injection when writing to the .env file.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit 8c1989e36ad2 changed the line handling .env updates to wrap values in double quotes without escaping existing quotes or sanitizing the input. This permitted an attacker to inject characters (like `"`) into the OAuth process, which the subsequent vulnerable `updateEnvFile` function then wrote into the .env file, leading to arbitrary configuration injection.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit changed the critical line from `${key}=${value}` to `${key}="${value}"`, and blame shows it authored the exact interpolation line removed by the fix. Although the helper was already unsafe before, this commit re-authored the sink in a still-vulnerable form: it continued concatenating unescaped token data into `.env`, now allowing quote-breaking payloads instead of addressing the underlying injection problem. Under the 'reimplementation is bug introduction' rule, this is a confirmed BIC for the vulnerable pre-fix state.

cves.ai_contribution: Yes. Claude-authored commit 4c9c826c6fc8b64a20e948cb46fefdda42d5244d introduced the unsafe `updateEnvFile()` helper and wired it into token-setting paths, causing caller-controlled token strings to be written directly into `.env` via string interpolation. Claude also co-authored 8c1989e36ad2950fcf4c8f9f95027ebf487e0f61, which rewrote the same sink to add quotes but still left values unescaped, so AI-authored/AI-assisted code was part of the causal chain.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code (Claude Opus 4.5/4.6 via Claude Code) introduced the core vulnerability across multiple commits. Commit 9f66c42f wrote the fragile `_is_wcu_exhausted()` method and the shard retry logic that lacked proper failure classification, allowing DynamoDB throttling to be misinterpreted. Commit 6bcd2606 implemented the proactive sharding mechanism using an incorrect signal (`wcu_tc_delta` consumption ratio instead of remaining token level) and wrote the deficient `propagate_shard_count` function that only updated `shard_count` on existing shards without pre-creating new shard items. The initial commit 3902c8c also contributed by writing `get_resource_capacity` without shard deduplication, which became exploitable once sharding was introduced.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted` logic and the initial handling of speculative failures related to shard limits, which the vulnerability analysis identifies as flawed. Specifically, this commit added the rudimentary check that failed to account for correct capacity aggregation and proper handling of DynamoDB throttling states, which the fix commit subsequently replaces with more granular `SpeculativeFailureReason` checks.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented `_is_wcu_exhausted` using a simplified logic check (comparing tokens_milli < 1000) that failed to correctly interpret DynamoDB partition throttling states. This logic error meant that when the system encountered high contention or throttling, it incorrectly triggered shard doubling or retries without proper deduplication or capacity validation, ultimately leading to the reported DoS condition.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted()` static method (lines 1006-1026 in limiter.py) which the fix removes entirely, replacing it with `SpeculativeFailureReason` enum matching. The method's fragile heuristic (checking `tokens_milli < 1000` from the limiter side) was insufficient to distinguish between app-limit exhaustion and wcu exhaustion. The commit also wrote the shard retry logic (line 745: `if result.shard_count > 1`) which unconditionally retried on other shards without checking whether the failure was app-limit or wcu-based — the fix adds `and result.failure_reason == SpeculativeFailureReason.APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`. This commit also added `bump_shard_count` to repository.py (without the warning threshold the fix adds). All blame lines confirm 9f66c42f as the author.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: Initial commit: zae-limiter rate limiting library

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This initial commit wrote the `get_resource_capacity()` method in limiter.py (lines 1875-1900, all blamed to 3902c8c). The method iterates over buckets without deduplicating by entity_id — it treats each bucket as a separate entity. The fix adds entity_id grouping, summing available tokens across shards and capping at capacity. While this code was correct pre-sharding (one bucket per entity), it became a vulnerability when sharding was introduced (94a129ae) — each shard was counted separately, inflating capacity. The commit authored the design flaw (no entity grouping) that the fix reverses.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: 🔒 fix(repository): migrate bucket items to per-shard partition keys

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the foundational logic for sharded bucket discovery (the `shard_id` parameter and GSI3-based lookups) which ultimately contained the flawed capacity accounting and insufficient throttle handling. By shifting the architecture to explicitly rely on sharded lookups without implementing the necessary logic to aggregate and deduplicate state across those shards or handle the resulting increased risk of partition-level throttling, this commit established the vulnerable pattern.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit implemented the multi-shard retrieval mechanism (e.g., using `shard_id` and GSI3 for discovery) but failed to provide the corresponding application-level logic to correctly aggregate and account for capacity across these newly introduced shards, setting the stage for the identified resource exhaustion vulnerabilities.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit migrated bucket items to per-shard partition keys, fundamentally changing the data model. It introduced the sharding infrastructure (pk_bucket, shard_id parameters, GSI3 for discovery) that made the pre-existing `get_resource_capacity` logic in 3902c8c exploitable. After this commit, `get_resource_buckets` returns multiple buckets per entity (one per shard), but `get_resource_capacity` treats each as a separate entity. This commit changed the calling context that made the latent weakness in capacity calculation exploitable. It's part of the causal chain for the DoS vulnerability.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(aggregator): filter wcu, proactive sharding, shard propagation

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit (6bcd260679f3) introduced the initial implementation of `try_proactive_shard` and its associated logic using `wcu_tc_delta` (consumption delta) as the metric for scaling. This logic was flawed because it monitored recent consumption deltas rather than actual token levels, failing to correctly account for sustained load, which directly matches the root cause described in the vulnerability analysis.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit implemented a proactive sharding mechanism that incorrectly relied on 'wcu_tc_delta' (recent consumption delta) to trigger scaling, rather than assessing sustained capacity depletion, leading to inadequate resource scaling under high-load scenarios.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit wrote the entire `try_proactive_shard()` function and `propagate_shard_count()` function in processor.py. The fix changes the proactive sharding signal from `wcu_tc_delta` (consumption delta ratio >= 0.8) to `wcu_tk_milli` (remaining token ratio < 0.2), a fundamentally different and more reliable metric. The old `propagate_shard_count` only did `UpdateItem` on shards 1..new_count with `attribute_not_exists(shard_count) OR shard_count < :new`, which just updated shard_count but didn't create full bucket items for newly created shards. The fix rewrites this into two paths: lightweight update for existing shards, and full PutItem with TypeDeserializer for new shards. Both flaws are directly attributable to this commit's code, confirmed by blame.

cves.ai_contribution: AI-authored code (Claude Opus 4.5/4.6 via Claude Code) introduced the core vulnerability across multiple commits. Commit 9f66c42f wrote the fragile `_is_wcu_exhausted()` method and the shard retry logic that lacked proper failure classification, allowing DynamoDB throttling to be misinterpreted. Commit 6bcd2606 implemented the proactive sharding mechanism using an incorrect signal (`wcu_tc_delta` consumption ratio instead of remaining token level) and wrote the deficient `propagate_shard_count` function that only updated `shard_count` on existing shards without pre-creating new shard items. The initial commit 3902c8c also contributed by writing `get_resource_capacity` without shard deduplication, which became exploitable once sharding was introduced.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Generic webhook channel trusts caller-supplied identity fields; allowlist is checked against untrusted payload data
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code contributed directly: commit 2c9deefbf744089c3041885717b92c6f2fc0bf8c (co-authored by Claude) introduced the webhook channel and its trust model, deserializing caller-supplied `sender`/`chat_id`, checking the allowlist against `payload.sender`, and publishing those untrusted fields into `InboundMessage`. The fix reverses that design by adding server-side `sender_id`/`chat_id` and making `trust_payload_identity` an explicit legacy escape hatch.
cves.root_cause: The webhook channel trusted user-supplied identity fields without proper validation, allowing attackers to spoof identities by passing untrusted payload data against the allowlist.
cves.vuln_type: Improper Authentication / Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Relying on caller-supplied identity fields in webhook payloads without verifying their authenticity or validating them against a trusted, server-side configuration.

bug_commits.author: qhkmdev90
bug_commits.message: feat: add hooks system, deploy templates, and accumulated features

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `WebhookChannel` implementation, including the initial data structure that parsed and trusted the `sender` and `chat_id` directly from the incoming JSON payload. This established the vulnerable pattern by design, as the initial implementation lacked any mechanism to validate these fields against server-side configuration.

In the new file, `WebhookPayload` required attacker-controlled `sender: String` and `chat_id: String`, `handle_connection()` enforced `base_config.is_allowed(&payload.sender)`, and `InboundMessage::new("webhook", payload.sender.trim(), payload.chat_id.trim(), payload.message.trim())` propagated those untrusted identity fields downstream. Pickaxe searches for `sender: String`, `chat_id: String`, `payload.sender.trim()`, `payload.chat_id.trim()`, and `message, sender, and chat_id must be non-empty` all trace back to this commit, confirming the screening hypothesis.

The fix reverses that design by adding server-side `sender_id`/`chat_id` and making `trust_payload_identity` an explicit legacy escape hatch.

cves.description: zeptoclaw has Shell allowlist-blocklist bypass via command/argument injection and file name wildcards
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-authored/co-authored commits directly introduced several of the vulnerable controls: d3480ca94087b74f110bb5b80fc8219b32c8b8b5 added the brittle `python/perl/ruby/node -c/-e` regexes, and 1712debbea60af6adf4a8a5939a43f7ef9a1ac16 added the first-token allowlist with the empty-allowlist bypass. The original AI-coauthored shell security module in 91f6c2bf98e40238ad4d175513f0ee400fd62068 also introduced naive literal substring matching for sensitive paths, which enabled wildcard bypasses and was later reimplemented in 7a2db595188c30e7ce662ae8ab0ab9f234d2b6c2.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to properly sanitize shell arguments, allowing attackers to bypass blocklists using argument permutation (specifically involving rm flags in Android).
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Permitting arbitrary flag permutation or insufficient filtering of command arguments that allows escaping intended blocklists.

bug_commits.author: qhkmdev90
bug_commits.message: security: fix 6 findings from code security review + pre-launch prep

cves.description: OpenClaw has a Trusted-proxy Control UI pairing bypass which allows unpaired node sessions
cves.how_introduced: AI-assisted code in commit 20523b918adff4feae378ac9965e204c56b6e3d8 (co-authored by Cursor) directly introduced the vulnerable trusted-proxy pairing-bypass logic. That commit added a new `trustedProxyAuthOk` fast path for Control UI connections without preserving the existing operator-only role gate, which enabled unpaired node-role sessions.
cves.root_cause: The system failed to properly validate pairing status for WebSocket connections, allowing unpaired node sessions to interact with the Trusted-proxy Control UI due to insufficient authorization checks.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Processing control-ui messages without verifying if the underlying node session is correctly paired/authorized.

screening_verification.vuln_description: The system failed to properly validate pairing status for WebSocket connections, allowing unpaired node sessions to interact with the Trusted-proxy Control UI due to insufficient authorization checks.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Processing control-ui messages without verifying if the underlying node session is correctly paired/authorized.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit implemented the 'trustedProxyAuthOk' logic in both message handling blocks without validating the user's role. Pickaxe searches for `trustedProxyAuthOk`, `params.isControlUi && params.trustedProxyAuthOk`, and `resolvedAuth.mode === "trusted-proxy"` in this pairing-bypass context all identify 20523b918 as the first introduction. In that commit, `evaluateMissingDeviceIdentity()` gained `if (params.isControlUi && params.trustedProxyAuthOk) return { kind: "allow" }` and `shouldSkipControlUiPairing()` gained `if (trustedProxyAuthOk) return true`, while `message-handler.ts` computed `trustedProxyAuthOk` without any role check. screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized user-controlled file paths or filenames processed by file management logic, coupled with inadequate access control checks on file system operations.

A flaw was found in mirror-registry where an authenticated user can trick the system into accessing unintended internal or restricted systems by providing malicious web addresses. 

When the application processes these addresses, it automatically follows redirects without verifying the final destination, allowing attackers to route requests to systems they should not have access to.

cves.description: OpenClaw versions prior to 2026.2.13 contain a denial of service vulnerability in webhook handlers that buffer request bodies without strict byte or time limits. Remote unauthenticated attackers can send oversized JSON payloads or slow uploads to webhook endpoints causing memory pressure and availability degradation.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The AI-assisted commit 5c2cb6c591e4b63c2df0549ad2202403256e2a96 (co-authored by Claude and flagged as claude_code) introduced Feishu webhook mode and registered `Lark.adaptDefault(path, eventDispatcher, { autoChallenge: true })` directly on an HTTP server without any body-size or body-time limits; the fix wraps that exact handler in `installRequestBodyLimitGuard`. Earlier AI-assisted Feishu commits only implemented websocket behavior or explicitly left webhook mode unimplemented, so they did not make the vulnerable request-body path reachable.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Email Sender Spoofing to bypass Header-Only From Allowlist Validation
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code directly introduced the email spoofing weakness. Commit 51bc07a02484ddfd2ec9c7f382dc43f829a9df86, marked as Claude Code-generated/co-authored, created the Email channel and `allowed_senders` feature that trusted the parsed RFC822 `From` header as sender identity without any SPF/DKIM/DMARC or equivalent authentication, which is exactly the trust-model flaw the fix later warns about.

bug_commits.author: qhkmdev90
bug_commits.message: feat(channels): add Email channel (IMAP IDLE + SMTP), feature-gated

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Pickaxe shows this is the first commit adding `pub struct EmailConfig`, `pub allowed_senders: Vec<String>`, `pub fn is_sender_allowed(&self, from: &str) -> bool`, the `parsed.from()` extraction, and `InboundMessage::new("email", &from, &from, &content)`. In that same commit, the new Email channel treated the parsed RFC822 `From` header as the authoritative sender identity for allowlist checks and session identity, with no SPF/DKIM/DMARC or other authentication logic anywhere in the code; bf004 later only adds warnings documenting that trust-model limitation. This commit therefore introduced the vulnerable design from the outset.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Pairing setup codes exposed long-lived shared gateway credentials instead of short-lived bootstrap tokens
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The original leak was introduced by human-authored setup-code generators, but AI-authored commit 130e59a9 added the iOS QR/deep-link onboarding path that parsed setup codes and persisted their embedded shared `token`/`password`, extending the insecure design into a first-class mobile pairing flow. That made AI code part of the causal chain even though it was not the first root introducer.

bug_commits.author: Hongwei Ma
bug_commits.message: feat: QR code scanning for gateway onboarding

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduces `GatewayConnectDeepLink`, which is a data transfer object for handling deep links, but it does not contain the logic for plugin auto-discovery or execution. The fix commit modifies `GatewayConnectDeepLink` to add a `bootstrapToken` field, which addresses authentication or verification for deep links, but the actual vulnerability (arbitrary code execution via workspace plugins) resides in a different part of the system responsible for plugin management, not this deep link parser.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application's plugin auto-discovery mechanism incorrectly trusted and executed code from repositories without proper validation or isolation, allowing untrusted code to run.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Automated loading and execution of plugins or configuration payloads from workspace directories without source verification.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced `GatewayConnectDeepLink` with `token`/`password` fields in `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawKit/DeepLinks.swift`, and its new iOS onboarding flow immediately stored those values from scanned setup codes (`handleScannedLink` sets `gatewayToken`/`gatewayPassword`). The fix changes exactly this path by adding `bootstrapToken` and clearing shared credentials when a bootstrap token is present. Even though the setup-code generator existed elsewhere, this AI-authored commit enabled the insecure shared-credential design in the QR onboarding path.

cves.description: Taylored webhook validation vulnerabilities
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-authored commit c139c021f68a09d22c2af88641b61c00f67f2af4 created the Taysell backend and crypto utilities with the core weaknesses: it trusted unsigned PayPal webhook bodies, allowed purchase-token reuse, used unsanitized patchId values in filesystem paths, and set PBKDF2 to 100000; the same AI release also omitted PAYPAL_WEBHOOK_ID from the setup flow, making secure webhook verification unavailable by default.

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the entire file `lib/handlers/buy-handler.ts` as a new feature (handling buy commands and polling for tokens). screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to properly validate incoming webhook signatures or payloads, allowing for potential request tampering or authentication bypasses.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Missing or insufficient cryptographic verification of incoming webhook requests before processing the payload.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The same c139 release introduced lib/handlers/setup-backend-handler.ts and wrote the generated .env with only PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID and PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET; there was no PAYPAL_WEBHOOK_ID prompt or env entry. A later follow-up (5e5a80b5) adds `paypalWebhookId` prompting and writes `PAYPAL_WEBHOOK_ID=${answers.paypalWebhookId}`, showing the original design shipped the payment backend without the credential needed for webhook verification. Under the causal-chain criteria, that omission is a confirmed enabler of the webhook-authentication weakness.
